title-group.article-title: Unlocking the therapeutic potential of immuno-radiotherapy: insights from preclinical and clinical research

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: The immunomodulatory potential of radiotherapy provides a strong rationale for its combination with immunotherapies and other immunostimulatory agents. However, current combinations yield suboptimal clinical benefits. As the mechanisms behind radiotherapy-induced immunomodulation are gradually unraveled, various key factors have been identified as critical modulators of this process, opening new possibilities for adapting radiotherapy to immunotherapy. Here, we discuss seven factors: dose‒fractionation regimens of radiotherapy (RT), sequence and timing of RT and immunotherapy, radiation field, type of ionizing radiation, normal tissue effects, choice of the lesion to irradiate and identification of biomarkers. Preclinical and clinical studies on the combination of immunotherapy and radiotherapy are discussed, novel immunotherapeutic approaches are highlighted and improvements in study design are suggested.

institution-wrap.institution: Fonds Alice et David van Buuren

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Cancer cells can evade immune responses,1 causing a need for therapies that potentiate antitumor immunity, including immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs). These antibodies restore the antitumor immune activity by blocking inhibitory interactions between cancer cells or antigen-presenting cells (APCs) and immune cells.2 ICIs targeting cytotoxic T lymphocyte-associated protein 4 (CTLA-4) and programmed cell death (ligand) 1 (PD-1/PD-L1) have become central to cancer treatment.3-6 However, durable responses remain limited due to tumor resistance.7 To improve outcomes, combination strategies with other immunostimulatory treatments are employed, including radiotherapy (RT).8 RT, historically regarded as a local treatment, is now recognized for its systemic immunomodulatory properties. These effects range from immunosuppression—such as lymphopenia,9-12 TGF-β-mediated promotion of anti-inflammatory immune cells,13,14 and increased expression of immune checkpoint molecules—to immunostimulation via induction of immunogenic cell death (ICD), activation of the cyclic GMP–AMP (cGAMP) synthase (cGAS)/stimulator of interferon (IFN) genes (STING) pathway and type I IFN signaling, reshaping of the cellular and cytokine/chemokine environment, and vasculature remodeling.15-18 The abscopal effect, defined as a regression of tumors outside the irradiation field, is a compelling example of RT-induced immunomodulation, though historically rare.19 The recent implementation of ICI in the clinic for patients receiving RT has significantly increased the occurrence of abscopal responses.20-24 This highlights the synergistic potential between RT and ICI, as RT can enhance tumor antigen presentation and immune infiltration, thereby priming the immune system for a more effective response to ICI. RT's immunomodulatory potential, broad accessibility25 and distinct toxicity profile relative to immunotherapy26-28 further support their combination. Together, these complementary modalities have led to the emergence of immuno-RT (iRT), a promising therapeutic approach that leverages the strengths of both treatments to improve patient outcomes. In this review, we highlight recent preclinical and clinical applications of iRT and discuss factors influencing its efficacy to optimize the design of future studies and trials.

sec.title: Preclinical evidence of iRT-mediated systemic immune activation
sec.p: Several immunotherapies have been investigated in combination with RT across various preclinical in vivo models, showing superior efficacy of iRT over monotherapies and reporting abscopal effects (Table 1). Besides RT combination with ICIs, other immunotherapies have been investigated, including adoptive cell therapy (ACT), vaccination, cytokines, chemokines and immunocytokines, and viruses. The benefits of these combinations surpass local tumor control and can yield systemic immune activation and abscopal effects, establish long-lasting immune memory protective against tumor rechallenge, known as “in situ vaccination”, induce complete responses (CRs) and extend survival.29 These preclinical studies enable the investigation of a wide range of experimental conditions that would be unfeasible in clinical settings. This allows the dissection of the mechanisms underlying iRT by identifying cellular mediators of the abscopal effect, such as CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, dendritic cells (DCs), and macrophages, thereby revealing novel immune targets beyond immune checkpoints. Yet, clinical translation remains challenging, partly due to animal model constraints, such as the limited diversity and relevance of available murine tumor models.

caption.p: Preclinical studies combining radiotherapy (RT) with different immunotherapies (IO), focusing on those showing abscopal responses or immune involvement in the antitumor response induced by the combination. All tumor models are murine unless specified otherwise. The table reports the effects of treatments besides local tumor control/regression.

fn.p: PD-1/PD-L1: programmed cell death (ligand) 1; MDSC: myeloid-derived suppressor cell; CR: complete response; DC: dendritic cell; CTLA-4: cytotoxic T lymphocyte-associated protein 4; Atg: autophagy; IFN: interferon; Casp: caspase; TREX1: activate three prime repair exonuclease 1; SCLC: small cell lung cancer; ACT: adoptive cell therapy; CAR: chimeric antigen receptor; ALL: acute lymphoblastic leukemia; HPV: human papillomavirus; CTGF: connective tissue growth factor; CEA: carcinoembryogenic antigen; HHP: high hydrostatic pressure; IL: interleukin; TGFβ: transforming growth factor beta; GzmB: granzyme B; MIP-1α: macrophage inflammatory protein-1 alpha; NK: natural killer; Flt3-L: Fms-like tyrosine kinase receptor 3-Ligand; STAT6 ASO: signal transducer and activator of transcription 6 (STAT6) antisense oligonucleotide; NLRP3: NACHT, LRR and PYD domain-containing protein 3; and H2O2: hydrogen peroxide.

sec.title: Roadmap for preclinical investigation of iRT abscopal effect
sec.p: Based on aforementioned preclinical studies, we propose a roadmap for optimal in vivo investigation of the abscopal effect and iRT efficacy (Figure 1).

caption.p: Roadmap for preclinical in vivo iRT studies: investigating the antitumor effects of immuno-radiotherapy require optimal study design. (1) Selection of a mouse model: immunocompetent, immunodeficient, or humanized. (2) Selection of a tumor model: genetically- or carcinogen-induced; cell-derived xenografts, which can be engrafted subcutaneously, intravenously, or orthotopically; or patient-derived xenografts. (3) Treatment selection based on clinical guidelines or preclinical studies for the appropriate dose and duration (immunotherapy) and characterization of cancer cell radiosensitivity by colony formation assay. (4) Monitoring of treatment effects macroscopically, reporting tumor growth and mouse survival. And microscopically reporting targets such as immunogenic cell death markers (DAMPs), cGAS/STING pathway activation and immune cells in the blood, tumors and lymphoid organs. Timing must be cautiously considered for microscopic monitoring. (5) Confirm observations of antitumor immunity by performing immune-depleting experiments using partially immunodeficient mice or depleting antibodies; performing rechallenge experiments with cancer cells; or ex vivo characterization of immune cell activity. GEMM: genetically engineered mouse model; CDX: cell-derived xenograft; PDX: patient-derived xenograft; DAMP: damage-associated molecular pattern; cGAS/STING: cyclic GMP–AMP synthase/stimulator of interferon genes; and Ab: antibody.

sec.p: While most iRT studies use immunocompetent or partially immunodeficient syngeneic mice, more advanced models have emerged. Humanized mice engrafted with a human immune system allow the in vivo study of human tumor–immune interactions. Unlike immunocompetent models, they support longer-term studies, owing to a slower tumor growth, and can be used with both cell-derived xenografts (CDXs) and patient-derived xenografts (PDXs).71-73 While most studies are conducted in allograft settings, PDX autograft models offer a path toward personalized medicine.74 In these models, mice are engrafted with a patient's immune system and matched tumor creating “clone mice” to his image.75-77 Yet humanized mice present limitations beyond their high cost and time-consuming nature, including incomplete immune reconstitution, lack of lymph nodes impairing T cell priming, allogeneic non-antigen-specific immune responses, and limited interactions between murine host cells and the human immune system or human tumor.74,77

For CDX studies, cancer cell lines should be selected based on their immunotype and ICI sensitivity, with intrinsic radiosensitivity assessed in vitro via colony formation assays. The linear quadratic (LQ) model, which yields parameters like the α/β ratio, can quantify radiosensitivity and allow biologically effective dose (BED) calculations for comparing different RT regimens.78,79 Although this model imperfectly predicts tumor radiosensitivity and does not consider tumor heterogeneity, it is still valued by radiation oncologists. In iRT studies, comparisons of the immunomodulatory effects of RT regimens of same BED are currently lacking.

Abscopal effects are often studied using bilateral tumor models, in which mice are engrafted with cancer cells in two separate regions, typically subcutaneously. Alternatives include intravenous injection of cancer cells, the use of orthotopic models, metastatic cancer cells, genetically engineered or carcinogen-induced models. These approaches provide diverse microenvironments, shaping environment-specific tumorigenesis, which can be determinant for treatment response, and the latter three better mimic progressive malignant development, making them more physiologically relevant than subcutaneously injected models. However, they still do not recapitulate the complexity of human metastases and present logistical challenges, requiring imaging techniques for treatment and monitoring.80-84 To address the challenge of the more rapid growth of non-irradiated tumors, secondary lesions can be engrafted later or with fewer cancer cells, delaying the maximum tolerated volume endpoint.29,85
Immunotherapy can be selected based on preclinically validated protocols or aligned with clinical practice, using interspecies dose conversions following existing guidelines.86

Immunomodulation can be monitored macroscopically by demonstrating abscopal effects and microscopically via immune profiling of tumors, blood and lymphoid organs.29,85 The timing of tissue sampling is challenging, as immune dynamics may not align with the final tumor response. Serial tumor sampling improves accuracy but increases animal use, raising ethical concerns. Alternatively, noninvasive imaging (i.e. PET) allows real-time live tracking of CD8+ T cell, using radiotracers like 89Zr-Df-IAB22M2C anti-CD8 minibody87,88 (NCT03802123) or 68Ga-NODAGA-SNA006 nanobody89 (NCT05126927), which were successfully used preclinically and in patients. Functional CD8+ T cells can also be visualized with radiotracers targeting Granzyme B, such as 68Ga-NOTA-GZP90 or 68Ga-grazytracer.91

To identify immune and nonimmune drivers of the abscopal effect, immunodeficient mice or depleting antibodies can be instrumental. Furthermore, rechallenge experiments evaluating the ability of the trained immune system to reject cancer cells can help detect long-lasting antitumor immunity. In addition, ex vivo characterization of T cells provides mechanistic insights, along with CR rates and overall survival (OS) of mice.85

To support reproducibility, transparent publication of both positive and negative results and relevant technical details such as beam energy or dose rate are necessary, although the latter are insufficiently reported.

Several key questions remain to be addressed in preclinical models to optimize the success of iRT (Figure 2), including which RT regimens should be used for which tumor types to achieve the desired immunostimulation while avoiding immunosuppression? When should immunotherapy be administered relative to RT? Should the entire tumor be irradiated at the risk of eliminating immune cells present in the TME? Should certain organs be spared from irradiation? Are all RT modalities equivalent in their immunomodulatory effects? Can irradiation of healthy tissue be leveraged to therapeutic advantage? Is there an order of priority in irradiating certain tumors over others in metastatic patients? And how can we identify the patients most likely to benefit from iRT? Answering these questions will require both preclinical and clinical efforts, and consistent, reproducible abscopal effects are unlikely to be achieved until they are fully addressed.

caption.p: Updated determining factors for optimal immuno-radiotherapy: optimal combinations of immuno-radiotherapy (iRT) rely on seven key factors, categorized into three axes: (A) treatment delivery: (1) dose-fractionation regimens of radiotherapy (RT) that promote antitumor immunity, spare circulating peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs), induce vascular remodeling while preventing the activation of immunosuppressive pathways, i.e. through three prime repair exonuclease 1 (TREX1) upregulation; (2) sequence and timing of RT and immunotherapy (IO), which may depend on the immunotherapeutic agent; (3) the radiation field that spares blood vessels and lymphoid organs, or the partial irradiation of the tumor with low-dose RT (LDRT) and with high-dose RT (HDRT) on another tumor region; (4) type of ionizing radiation and their characteristics, such as dose-rate or beam energy. (B) Irradiated tissue parameters: (5) RT effects on normal tissue, including toxicity and benefits depending on the tissue; (6) choice of the lesion to irradiate, considering lesion immunotypes, clonality, and host organs, and the possibility to irradiate multiple lesions. (C) Patient selection: (7) identification of biomarkers predictive of iRT response.

sec.title: Determining factors for optimal iRT combination
Dose-fractionation regimens
sec.p: The immunostimulatory effects of RT rely both on the total dose and the delivery scheme but do not increase linearly with the dose (Figure 3). RT is typically delivered by fractionating the total dose. Conventional fractionated RT (CFRT) consists of doses of 2 Gy per fraction for a total dose of 30–80 Gy, depending on the indication.92 Stereotactic body RT (SBRT) uses fewer fractions at higher doses, usually above 5 Gy per fraction.93

caption.p: Effects of the dose–fractionation of radiotherapy on the tumor microenvironment: radiotherapy-mediated immunomodulation depends on the regimen used, with doses per fraction varying between LDRT, CFRT, MD-SBRT and HD-SBRT. All of which can induce proinflammatory and anti-inflammatory effects by recruiting or polarizing immune cells in the tumor or by regulating signaling pathways. CFRT is particularly efficient to normalize tumor vasculature, decreasing hypoxia and facilitating immune cell recruitment. MD-SBRT is the preferred range to induce ICD with the release of DAMPs, TAAs, cytokines, chemokines, and activation of the cGAS/STING pathway. Although it induces ICD, HD-SBRT disrupts the vasculature and induces endothelial cell death. Above a certain threshold, HD-SBRT activates TREX1, which impairs type I IFN signaling. RT: radiotherapy; LDRT: low-dose radiotherapy; CFRT: conventional fractionated radiotherapy; MD-SBRT: moderate-dose stereotactic body radiotherapy; HD-SBRT: high-dose stereotactic body radiotherapy; fct: fraction; Th: T helper; Treg: T regulatory; NK: natural killer; DC: dendritic cell; IFN: interferon; IL: interleukin; TGF: transforming growth factor; HIF: hypoxia inducible factor; Arg: arginase; TAA: tumor-associated antigen; ICD: immunogenic cell death; DAMPs: damage-associated molecular patterns; cGAS/STING: cyclic GMP‒AMP synthase/stimulator of interferon genes; TNF: tumor necrosis factor; and TREX1: three prime repair exonuclease 1.

sec.p: Low-dose RT (LDRT) (<2 Gy per fraction) was shown to induce inflammation in preclinical models via IFN-α and IFN-γ responses, complement activation, interleukin (IL)-6/JAK/STAT3 signaling and suppression of transforming growth factor (TGF)-β1 signaling.94-96 It also increased tumor levels of activated CD8+ and CD4+ T cells, natural killer (NK) cells, CD11b+ myeloid cells, DCs and antitumor macrophages,94,95,97 although macrophage polarization is a dynamic process and better examined on a spectrum rather than as binary states.98 In patients with poorly infiltrated tumors, LDRT increased T cell levels, in particular CD4+ T helper (Th) 1 cells in responders, while protumor macrophages and neutrophils were increased in non-responders.94

CFRT is known to normalize blood vessels, increase vascular coverage causing tumor reoxygenation, and decrease hypoxia, thereby favoring an inflammatory microenvironment.97,99-101 This, together with nitric oxide (NO) production, and downregulation of protumor macrophage associated factors, can trigger the polarization of tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) toward the antitumor phenotype and increase the recruitment of CD8+ T and T regulatory (Treg) cells.32,33,97,101

Hypofractionated moderate-dose (MD)-SBRT (<10 Gy per fraction), is considered optimal for inducing abscopal effects as it is strongly pro-inflammatory. It has been shown to promote tumor infiltration by CD8+ T cells, DCs, NK cells, macrophages and neutrophils, to stimulate cytokine production,46,58,60,102-104 to induce ICD105 and to activate cGAS/STING pathway,46 while preserving circulating lymphocytes.106,107 However, MD-SBRT can also contribute to immunosuppression, by promoting the tumor infiltration of immunosuppressive monocytes, protumor TAMs, Tregs and tumor associated neutrophils (TANs).60,108 Additionally, it may enhance tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-α signaling and caspase 3 activation, which can suppress type I IFN signaling, thereby dampening the antitumor immune response.44,58,109,110

High dose (HD)-SBRT (>10 Gy per fraction) disrupt tumor vasculature by inducing endothelial cell death, impairing immune cell recruitment through vessels111 and increasing protumor macrophage polarization.112 These doses also activate three prime repair exonuclease 1 (TREX1), preventing cGAS/STING pathway activation, decreasing immune cell recruitment and local tumor control.45,113 Yet, HD-SBRT was also shown to induce ICD and tumor-associated antigen (TAA) release while increasing CD8+ T and Treg cell levels in tumors.114,115

Currently, there is no consensus on an optimal regimen to synergize with immunotherapy (Figure 3). Differences in the reported benefits of iRT, whether favoring a single fraction115-117 or multiple fractions,46,102 likely reflect variability in tumor models, the translational gap between preclinical and clinical settings, the immunotherapeutic agent used, and study design, often comparing total doses rather than regimens with equal BED.78 Some studies recommend hybrid regimens, starting with a high ablative RT dose, followed by LDRT to prevent RT-induced immune cell death and organ damage.118 Others suggest using both LDRT and high-dose SBRT on different tumor sites in metastatic settings, in combination with immunotherapy. This approach takes advantage of immunostimulatory and cell-killing effects of both LDRT and HD-SBRT and can enhance abscopal effects.37,95,119-123 Overall, the dual immunomodulatory effects of different RT regimens underscore the need for further preclinical and clinical studies to better define the optimal regimen and combination strategies that maximize antitumor immunity while minimizing immunosuppression. Moreover, a comprehensive characterization of cancer cells, TME, the vasculature and the oxygen status prior to irradiation is essential to understand these effects.124

sec.title: Sequence of immunotherapy and RT
sec.p: The sequence of immunotherapy and RT is central to achieve effective immune activation,125 and likely depends on the RT regimen and immunotherapeutic agent used, with most current data relating to ICIs.

First, RT can induce changes in the expression of checkpoint molecules on the surface of cancer cells and immune cells. For example, RT generally increases the expression of PD-L1 on the surface of cancer cells and immune cells across different cancer types, thereby making tumors more susceptible to respond to subsequent ICI.17,126

Then, RT-induced immunostimulation, such as adjuvant release during ICD, occurs within different timeframes. For example, calreticulin exposure on the cell surface increases over 12, 24 and 72 h post-RT,127 while heat shock protein 70 (HSP70) and high mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) only start increasing after 24 and 48 h, respectively.128 The subsequent recruitment and activation of APCs and T cell priming depends on these dynamics, suggesting that ICI administered during or after RT takes advantage of the peak immune stimulation to foster a potent antitumor response,129-131 as opposed to adjuvant ICI acting on suboptimally-stimulated immune cells.

Several preclinical studies support administering ICI after RT, as this sequencing was shown to increase antigen-specific T-cell priming compared to administering ICI before RT.132 In addition, evidence suggests that administering anti-PD-1 prior to RT radiosensitizes CD8+ T cells, abrogating the systemic effects of the combination.34 Others have described survival benefits and antitumor immune responses to CFRT combined with ICIs when given concurrently or immediately after RT, but not when ICIs were administered 7 d post RT, indicating a possible optimal time window.101,126

In clinical settings however, optimal iRT timing is not as clear, potentially revealing tumor type-specific differences. Several trials have demonstrated the benefits of iRT over standard of care in neoadjuvant settings, with SBRT given before ICI,21,129,133 while others are prone to giving ICI before and during RT.134,135

Determining the optimal timing of immunotherapy and RT warrants further investigation, and as RT induces T cell clonal expansion both within tumors and systemically,136 monitoring T cell numbers and activation status in the peripheral blood of patients after RT could indicate the optimal time to start immunotherapy, ensuring maximum therapeutic benefit.17,137

sec.title: Radiation field
sec.p: While RT can prime systemic antitumor immune responses, it can also cause immune cell depletion. Limiting irradiation of the blood, spleen, lymph nodes (LNs) and Peyer's patches or part of the tumors is thus instrumental to enhance iRT's efficacy.132,138-140

Systemic immune cell alterations were described when exposing the spleen to RT.141-143 Similarly, sparing tumor-draining lymph nodes (TDLNs) during RT was shown to enhance antitumor immune responses.144 This has been observed when RT was administered alone (112) or combined with anti-PD-(L)1,132,145,146 anti-CTLA-4,147 anti-CTLA-4 + anti-PD-1 (116) or Treg depleting agents like anti-CD25.145 Irradiation or surgical removal of TDLNs impaired the local and systemic efficacy of iRT by decreasing T cell activation and trafficking.144,145,147-149 Delayed irradiation or surgical dissection of TDLNs has emerged as a promising strategy to maintain their immunological function while reducing the risk of local recurrence.145 In addition, RT regimens delivered in fewer fractions and to smaller volumes such as SBRT are more prone to preserve circulating lymphocytes as opposed to CFRT.106,150,151

Partial tumor irradiation allows the use of high doses, distributed spatially in the tumor rather than temporally. Different techniques have emerged, such as spatially fractionated GRID RT (SFGRT) or lattice radiation therapy (LRT).

Partial tumor volume irradiation can provide tumor control similar to whole-tumor irradiation.152 This effect involves immune stimulation, which can lead to abscopal effects both in preclinical models and in patients.152,153

Partial irradiation can also be used to obtain heterogeneous intratumor irradiation doses, combining LDRT and HDRT in different tumor regions.154 Although more technically challenging, this offers an alternative when full tumor HDRT is not feasible. In murine models, two half-tumor irradiations with 2 Gy and 16 Gy combined with anti-PD-1 antibodies produced similar tumor control effects as 16 Gy whole-tumor irradiation, while increasing the tumor immune cell infiltration and activation and mouse survival,154 suggesting that it efficiently combined the benefits of high- and low-dose RT.

SFGRT is a two-dimensional RT technique delivering high doses nonuniformly to the tumor by filtering the beam into a grid-like pattern. In a murine triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) model treated with whole-tumor irradiation or SFGRT, only the latter induced an abscopal response, increased tumor inflammation and IFN-γ signaling.155

Lattice RT consists in three-dimensional delivery of high-dose radiation to islands within the tumor, improving normal tissue sparing compared to SFGRT.156 When comparing different irradiation volumes of LRT in murine lung carcinoma tumors, 100% and 2% × 10% tumor volume irradiation delayed tumor growth. Abscopal effects were induced by all volumes of irradiation (2% × 10%, 20%, 50% and 100%). Similar to SFGRT, LRT triggered a proinflammatory immune response characterized by IFN-γ signaling.157
In this context, modalities capable of delivering highly spatially heterogeneous dose distributions, such as proton therapy or brachytherapy, also warrant further investigation.158,159
Taken together, these results support the preservation of blood, spleen and LNs as well as partial-tumor irradiation to enhance systemic and intratumoral antitumor immune responses.

sec.title: Type of ionizing radiation
sec.p: Most data on the immunomodulatory effects of RT derives from studies using photon-based radiation, which is the most commonly applied form of radiation in clinical and preclinical settings. However, other radiation modalities, such as charged particle radiation, including protons and carbon ions, may have the potential to induce antitumor immune responses, which can be further enhanced by combining them with immunotherapy.160,161

While controlled studies comparing these modalities with photon irradiation are lacking, both proton and carbon-ion irradiation were shown to induce the release of damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs),162-164 and induce abscopal responses.165,166 In addition, owing to its improved dose distribution, proton RT was shown to decrease the occurrence of lymphopenia in glioblastoma patients compared to photon RT.167 Further research is needed to compare these different treatment modalities in the context of immunomodulation and combinations with immunotherapy to define the settings in which charged particle therapy is superior to photon irradiation.

Other characteristics also influence the effect of radiation, such as the dose-rate. Ultra-high dose rate FLASH RT has been shown to better spare healthy tissue such as the brain and intestine while maintaining tumor control.168,169 However, other tissues do not seem to benefit from the FLASH-sparing effect, including the blood compartment, as FLASH has been shown to induce leukopenia equally to conventional dose-rate irradiation.170 FLASH and conventional dose-rate irradiation similarly induced immune-independent tumor control and microenvironment remodeling in mice, with a decrease in lymphoid cells and an increase in myeloid cells.171 In contrast, in a murine medulloblastoma model, FLASH proton beam radiation induced proinflammatory polarization of macrophages, increased T cell infiltration in tumors, and enhanced the chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cell response.172 Ultra-high dose rate could thus be a promising modality for immunotherapy combinations, although further research is needed.

Another factor influencing the cellular response to RT is the beam energy. The energy used in the clinic is in the MV range (usually 6–10 MV), while preclinical microirradiator beams are using energies in the range of kV (usually 225 kV), which is known to be associated with slightly higher relative biological effectiveness (RBE).173 To our knowledge, studies investigating the immunological effects of different energy X-rays are currently lacking but may be of importance to the field.
This highlights the need for a multidisciplinary approach to study design and to thoroughly report irradiation parameters in publications to facilitate reproducibility and translatability.

sec.title: Normal tissue exposure
sec.p: RT-induced inflammation occurs both in the tumor and in the surrounding healthy tissues. This can cause adverse effects, but it can also potentiate the iRT response.

RT-induced adverse events are partly due to the direct cytotoxicity of radiation but also to the RT-induced immune response.16,174 For example, lung irradiation was associated with infiltration of Th1, Th2, and Treg cells; antitumor and protumor macrophages and neutrophils, which can cause alveolar edema or occlusion; and pulmonary fibrosis.175 Gut irradiation can damage the intestinal tissue, leading to microbiota leakage and inflammation. This is characterized by an accumulation of macrophages, neutrophils, eosinophils and Th2 cells, which sustain inflammation, induce fibrosis, and hamper tissue regeneration.16 RT-induced normal tissue cell death has also been shown to generate antigen-specific autoimmunity, for example, thyroid irradiation leading to thyroiditis,176,177 and breast irradiation inducing autoimmune reactions.178

Immunotherapy may enhance the deleterious immune effects on healthy tissue. In the PACIFIC trial, pneumonitis occurrence was increased in patients treated with ICI and RT compared to RT alone.130 The effects on healthy tissue must be carefully monitored to ensure patient safety in iRT trials.

However, some studies indicate that healthy tissue irradiation can enhance iRT benefits. In an orthotopic murine non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC) model, lung irradiation induced club cell proliferation and the secretion of immunostimulatory cytokines, taking part in iRT efficacy.179 These benefits may rely on the cytokines secreted after healthy tissue irradiation or the killing of immunosuppressive cells.180 Recently, intestinal low-dose RT (ILDR) has been shown to improve the OS of advanced cancer patients treated with ICI or immunogenic chemotherapy. These findings were validated in a murine model of colon cancer treated with ILDR and anti-PD-L1. ILDR (1–3 Gy) enhanced response to anti-PD-L1 in unirradiated tumors, an effect relying on the presence of Christensenella minuta in the gut microbiota. These bacteria enhanced the efficacy of ILDR and anti-PD-L1 combination, facilitating the migration of DCs from mesenteric LNs to TDLNs. Additionally, ILDR has shown promising results to overcome patient anti-PD-1 resistance.181
These studies underscore the opportunity to balance the detrimental and beneficial effects of healthy tissue inflammation following iRT.

sec.title: Identifying the optimal lesion to irradiate
sec.p: iRT is often given to patients with metastatic cancer, and RT can be delivered to one or multiple metastatic sites. However, each metastasis is defined by its clonality, immunotype and host organ, which affect the response to iRT.

Metastatic clones are characterized by point mutations or somatic copy number alterations including chromosome aneuploidies, and therefore present different tumor neoantigen loads, immunogenicity and responses to therapies compared to the primary tumor.182,183 While point mutations increase the neoantigen load, the aneuploidy score inversely correlates with tumor immunogenicity, they could thus be determinants of the iRT response.184 Based on these differences, irradiating most tumor lesions may diversify the released antigens and potentiate the immune response.

Tumor immunotypes depend on their origin and localization,185 and determine the response to RT186 and immunotherapy.187 The influence of the localization is seen for example in brain tumors which generally present low immune cell infiltration with a majority of macrophages, while lung carcinoma and melanoma are usually highly infiltrated, dominated by T lymphocytes.188-190 In a murine breast cancer model, combined RT and anti-PD-1 synergistically suppressed extracranial but not intracranial metastases, where brain neutrophils and macrophages mediated local immunosuppression.191 Another example is the liver, in which metastases were shown to siphon tumor-specific CD8+ T cells and induce their apoptosis through hepatic macrophages, leading to their systemic depletion and immunotherapy resistance. Liver metastasis irradiation was shown to eliminate the macrophages, restoring the response to immunotherapy.192 The tissue-specific immune microenvironment can thus shape the response to iRT.

Finally, (oligo)metastatic patients may benefit from irradiation to all lesions when combined with ICI,22,119,193 potentially yielding survival benefits through total tumor load reduction and immune activation. Importantly, RT was shown to increase distant unirradiated metastatic growth through amphiregulin signaling, which mediated cancer cell proliferation and myeloid-tolerogenic reprogramming, highlighting the limitations of single-lesion irradiation and further supporting the irradiation of all metastases.194,195
These studies highlight the importance of characterizing irradiated lesions for iRT treatment planning in metastatic patients.

sec.title: Predictive biomarkers for iRT response
sec.p: Although some predictive biomarkers for the response to ICI or RT have been identified, they may not adequately predict the response to the combination.180,196,197 For instance, the tumor expression level of PD-L1 is a well-established predictive biomarker for anti-PD-1/PD-L1 response across multiple tumor types, but its utility in the context of iRT remains uncertain.198 Indeed, RT can increase tumor PD-L1 expression,30 potentially making the elevated baseline PD-L1 level dispensable for effective iRT. This was observed in NSCLC patients in which chemoRT and durvalumab improved progression-free survival (PFS) regardless of the baseline PD-L1 expression level130 and in the Pembro-RT trial, in which, compared to ICI alone, iRT increased PFS in PD-L1-negative NSCLC patients.199

The cGAS/STING signaling pathway has emerged as a potential predictive factor of iRT response. Some cancers exhibit impaired cGAS/STING signaling,200,201 potentially hindering RT-mediated type I IFN release.45 Baseline levels of cGAS and STING could thus help identifying patients who might benefit from iRT.

Another candidate biomarker is the aneuploidy score of tumors, as highly aneuploid tumors are associated with immune suppression and reduced responses to ICIs183 but may exhibit synergistic benefits when ICIs are combined with RT.184,202

A promising negative predictive biomarker is TREX1, an exonuclease that prevents the activation of the cGAS/STING pathway. Baseline elevated TREX1 levels can prevent tumor-specific CD8+ T cell development and impair iRT-mediated tumor regression and abscopal effects.45 Since HDRT upregulates TREX1, its level in tumors post-RT may indicate possible benefits of subsequent immunotherapy, with high TREX1 levels correlating with poor antitumor immune activation and tumor control.45,113

Similarly, tumor levels of DAMPs, effector immune cells, cytokines and chemokines are biomarker candidates. Pre- and post-RT evaluation of the tumor immune infiltrate can be performed on biopsies or by noninvasive radiomics or imaging techniques. Indeed, in patients presenting with advanced solid tumors treated with iRT, a higher baseline tumor CD8 radiomics score correlated with an increased response to iRT.203 Importantly, the spatial distribution of immune cells in tumors defines the interactions among immune cells and between immune and cancer cells, orchestrating the response to immunotherapy.204 Although further evidence is needed to establish the predictive value of TME spatial distribution for iRT, the clinical implementation of multiplexing immunohistochemistry, spatial transcriptomics or spatial proteomics will be informative.

DAMPs, cytokines and chemokines can also be monitored in the blood. Circulating levels of lymphocytes, or absolute lymphocyte count before or after RT, have also been correlated with abscopal responses in patients treated with iRT.24,205 If further validated, circulating biomarker detection may provide a safer and cheaper approach for patient selection.196

Nontumor biomarkers are also emerging, such as the gut microbiome, which regulates ICI responses through specific bacteria.206-209 Gut microbiome disruption by antibiotics has been shown to influence the response to RT. Vancomycin, which favors Akkermansia at the expense of Gram + bacteria, was evidenced to enhance CD8+ T cell priming and IFNγ signaling after RT and increased local and abscopal tumor control.210 This suggests that the gut microbiome and antibiotic treatments could be predictive of iRT response and should be monitored in clinical trials.
Overall, there is a significant need for validated biomarkers predictive of response to iRT to improve patient selection, clinical trial outcomes and personalized treatment strategies.

sec.title: Clinical applications of iRT
sec.p: Encouraging preclinical results have led to numerous clinical trials investigating iRT, primarily combining RT with ICIs to assess safety and efficacy. While these trials have confirmed the safety and therapeutic benefits of iRT over monotherapy,130,211,212 clinical evidence of abscopal responses remains rare, mostly confined to case reports.213 This may be attributed to the factors discussed in Determining factors for optimal iRT combination (Figure 2).

The TONIC trial tested five priming strategies before starting anti-PD-1 in metastatic TNBC: no priming; 3 × 8 Gy to one metastasis; cyclophosphamide; and cisplatin or doxorubicin. The best overall response rates were achieved with doxorubicin, while RT showed no advantage over no priming.214 Despite several suboptimal aspects of the TONIC trial design, such as the sequential delivery of RT and ICI with a two-week interval, low patient numbers, and targeting RT to only one metastasis in a polymetastatic setting, the conclusions of the trial indicated that translating preclinical results to humans would not be straightforward. Similarly, the CHEERS trial, directing 3 × 8 Gy to up to 3 metastases in different tumor types in combination with ICI, showed no survival benefit of iRT over ICI. Notably, in 30% of patients in the experimental arm, SBRT targeted metastatic LNs.215

In contrast, the Pembro-RT trial reported a significantly higher out-of-field response when SBRT (3 × 8 Gy) was combined with pembrolizumab, suggesting that iRT benefits patients with metastatic NSCLC, with the best response observed in the PD-L1-negative subgroup.199,212 This subgroup with immunologically cold tumors had significantly longer PFS in the SBRT + pembrolizumab arm. In addition, cold tumors had significantly more enriched IFN-gamma, IFN-alpha and antigen processing and presentation gene sets in nonirradiated tumor sites.199 However, further validation is needed before implementing this novel treatment strategy in clinical routine. Overall, in the metastatic setting, iRT is safe, but consistent evidence of abscopal effects is lacking.

The early (nonmetastatic) setting differs fundamentally to the metastatic setting, as iRT combinations may serve as an “in situ” vaccine to help prevent the development of metastases. However, trials combining immunotherapy with conventional RT approaches (fractionated with large fields and including elective LN irradiation) were mostly unable to demonstrate a benefit of iRT.216-219 In NSCLC for example, while the PACIFIC trial demonstrated improved OS when immunotherapy followed termination of chemoradiation,129 the PACIFIC-2 trial found no benefit of concomitant treatment.216 Similarly, the KEYNOTE-867 and NRG-LU005 trials conducted in lung cancer showed no benefits of ICI concurrent with (chemo)RT.220,221 Multiple phase III trials, such as the JAVELIN head and neck 100 and CheckMate-548 in glioblastoma and CALLA in cervical cancer found no benefits of chemoRT combined with concomitant followed by maintenance ICI against chemoRT alone.218,219,222 These results imply that when immunotherapy is given concomitantly with RT, conventional RT approaches should be questioned, as discussed previously in the preclinical setting. A suggested approach is to avoid elective nodal RT or to irradiate only the primary cancer concomitantly with immunochemotherapy while delaying nodal irradiation after surgery.21,180,223 In NSCLC, this approach has led to the downstaging of unirradiated LNs metastases,21 and in breast cancer, it is currently being tested in the P-RAD (NCT04443348) and Neo-CheckRay phase II randomized trials.224-227 During the preoperative phase, RT is directed exclusively toward the primary breast cancer, concomitantly with immunochemotherapy, while the treatment of the elective LNs, the uninvolved breast or the chest wall is completed in the postoperative phase without ICI. The P-RAD trial uses an immunoscore based on the TIL level on a biopsy two weeks post-RT as a coprimary endpoint with the response at surgery in nonirradiated axillary LNs to measure the abscopal nodal effect of adding RT to the primary in the neoadjuvant setting. The PEARL trial, investigating preoperative pembrolizumab combined with 3 × 8 Gy to treat primary tumors in patients with early-stage TNBC, demonstrated that the combination was feasible and effective. The trial reported a significant decrease in TILs and a promising pathologic complete response (pCR) rate.228 In cervical cancer and soft tissue sarcoma, positive trials reported on PFS when pembrolizumab was added to RT.229,230 Positive results have emerged from adding immunotherapy when SBRT is used as a curative modality without the use of surgery. An increase in event-free survival was reported when nivolumab was added to SBRT in four fractions directed to a primary NSCLC.231 In rectal cancer, concurrent ICI improved the 3-y OS when added to total neoadjuvant therapy.232 Overall, the clinical benefits of iRT are variable and require further adjustments as discussed in the previous sections, but some trials, mostly in the nonmetastatic setting, suggest a promising future for iRT.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Growing preclinical and clinical evidence supports the synergy between immunotherapy and RT, yielding local and systemic benefits, including durable antitumor immunity and abscopal responses. However, the clinical benefits remain modest compared to preclinical findings. Bridging this gap requires next-generation preclinical models and a deeper understanding of the biological mechanisms driving iRT efficacy. Emerging modulators, such as the gut microbiome and tumor aneuploidy, add further complexity to iRT optimization.

Key research priorities fall into three main axes (Figure 2): (A) optimizing treatment delivery: RT regimen, sequence with ICI, field and type of RT; (B) optimizing irradiated tissue parameters: normal tissue effect, and choice of irradiated lesion; and (C) optimizing patient selection through biomarker identification. Addressing these priorities will ultimately help resolve two major questions in the field: how can RT be harnessed to stimulate the immune system while avoiding immune suppression, and what are reliable markers of iRT response?

A major preclinical limitation remains the reliance on murine models and their restricted repertoire of cancer cell lines. Although imperfect, humanized mice represent an advancement in immuno-oncology, enabling in vivo studies of human immune‒tumor interactions and the use of heterogeneous PDXs.

Challenges ahead include the multitude of variables requiring optimization, the difficulty of interpreting iRT combination effects against monotherapies, balancing RT's dual role as a cytotoxic agent and immunomodulator, interference with other therapies, and the dynamic nature of antitumor immunity. The latter, still insufficiently explored, may be pivotal in unlocking the full potential of iRT.
